title: Leonardo da Vinci

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Italian polymath (1452–1519)

"Da Vinci" redirects here. For other uses, see [Da Vinci (disambiguation)](/wiki/Da_Vinci_(disambiguation) "Da Vinci (disambiguation)") and [Leonardo da Vinci (disambiguation)](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci_(disambiguation) "Leonardo da Vinci (disambiguation)").

| Leonardo da Vinci | |
| --- | --- |
| This portrait attributed to [Francesco Melzi](/wiki/Francesco_Melzi "Francesco Melzi"), c. 1515–1518, is the only certain contemporary depiction of Leonardo.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZöllner201920-2) | |
| Born | Leonardo di ser Piero da Vinci  (1452-04-15)15 April 1452  possibly [Anchiano](/wiki/Anchiano "Anchiano"),[[a]](#cite_note-Birthplace-3) [Vinci](/wiki/Vinci,_Tuscany "Vinci, Tuscany"), [Florence](/wiki/Republic_of_Florence "Republic of Florence") |
| Died | 2 May 1519(1519-05-02) (aged 67)  [Clos Lucé](/wiki/Clos_Luc%C3%A9 "Clos Lucé"), [Amboise](/wiki/Amboise "Amboise"), France |
| Resting place | [Château d'Amboise](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_d%27Amboise "Château d'Amboise") |
| Education | Studio of [Andrea del Verrocchio](/wiki/Andrea_del_Verrocchio "Andrea del Verrocchio") |
| Years active | c. 1470–1519 |
| Known for | * Painting * drawing * engineering * anatomical studies * hydrology * botany * optics * geology |
| Notable work | * + *[Virgin of the Rocks](/wiki/Virgin_of_the_Rocks "Virgin of the Rocks")* (c. 1483–1493)   + *[Lady with an Ermine](/wiki/Lady_with_an_Ermine "Lady with an Ermine")* (c. 1489–1491)   + *[The Vitruvian Man](/wiki/Vitruvian_Man "Vitruvian Man")* (c. 1490)   + *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")* (c. 1495–1498)   + *[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")* (c. 1503–1516) |
| Movement | [High Renaissance](/wiki/High_Renaissance "High Renaissance") |
| Family | [Da Vinci](/wiki/Da_Vinci_family "Da Vinci family") |

**Leonardo di ser Piero da Vinci**[[b]](#cite_note-IPA-4)[[c]](#cite_note-5) (15 April 1452 – 2 May 1519) was an Italian [polymath](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath") of the [High Renaissance](/wiki/High_Renaissance "High Renaissance") who was active as a painter, draughtsman, engineer, scientist, theorist, sculptor, and architect.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2003-6) While his fame initially rested on his achievements as a painter, he has also become known for [his notebooks](#Journals_and_notes), in which he made drawings and notes on a variety of subjects, including anatomy, astronomy, botany, cartography, painting, and [palaeontology](/wiki/Palaeontology "Palaeontology"). Leonardo is widely regarded as a genius who epitomised the [Renaissance humanist](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") ideal,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeydenreich2020-7) and his [collective works](/wiki/List_of_works_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci "List of works by Leonardo da Vinci") contributed to the development of European art to an extent rivalled only by that of his younger contemporary [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2003-6)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeydenreich2020-7)

Born out of wedlock to a successful notary and a lower-class woman in, or near, [Vinci](/wiki/Vinci,_Tuscany "Vinci, Tuscany"), he was educated in [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence") by the Italian painter and sculptor [Andrea del Verrocchio](/wiki/Andrea_del_Verrocchio "Andrea del Verrocchio"). He began his career in the city, but then spent much time in the service of [Ludovico Sforza](/wiki/Ludovico_Sforza "Ludovico Sforza") in Milan. Later, he worked in Florence and Milan again, as well as briefly in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), all while attracting [a large following](/wiki/Leonardeschi "Leonardeschi") of imitators and students. Upon the invitation of [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France"), he spent his last three years in France, where he died in 1519. Since his death, there has not been a time when his achievements, diverse interests, [personal life](/wiki/Personal_life_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Personal life of Leonardo da Vinci"), and empirical thinking have failed to incite interest and admiration,[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2003-6)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeydenreich2020-7) making him a frequent [namesake](/wiki/List_of_things_named_after_Leonardo_da_Vinci "List of things named after Leonardo da Vinci") and [subject in culture](/wiki/Cultural_references_to_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Cultural references to Leonardo da Vinci").

Leonardo is identified as one of the greatest painters in the history of [Western art](/wiki/Art_of_Europe "Art of Europe") and is often credited as the founder of the High Renaissance.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2003-6) Despite having many [lost works](/wiki/Lost_artworks "Lost artworks") and [fewer than 25 attributed major works](/wiki/List_of_works_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci#Major_extant_works "List of works by Leonardo da Vinci") – including numerous [unfinished works](/wiki/Unfinished_creative_work "Unfinished creative work") – he created some of the most influential paintings in the [Western canon](/wiki/Western_canon#Visual_arts "Western canon").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2003-6) The *[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")* is his best known work and is regarded as the world's most famous individual painting.[[5]](#cite_note-8) *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")* is the most reproduced religious painting of all time and his *[Vitruvian Man](/wiki/Vitruvian_Man "Vitruvian Man")* drawing is also regarded as a cultural icon. In 2017, *[Salvator Mundi](/wiki/Salvator_Mundi_(Leonardo) "Salvator Mundi (Leonardo)")*, attributed in whole or part to Leonardo,[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZöllner2019250-9) was sold at auction for US$450.3 million, setting a new record for the [most expensive painting ever sold](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_paintings "List of most expensive paintings") at public auction.

Revered for his [technological ingenuity](/wiki/Science_and_inventions_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Science and inventions of Leonardo da Vinci"), he conceptualised flying machines, a type of [armoured fighting vehicle](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_fighting_vehicle "Leonardo's fighting vehicle"), concentrated solar power, a ratio machine that could be used in an [adding machine](/wiki/Adding_machine "Adding machine"),[[7]](#cite_note-AddingMachine-10)[[8]](#cite_note-11) and the [double hull](/wiki/Double_hull "Double hull"). Relatively few of his designs were constructed or were even feasible during his lifetime, as the modern scientific approaches to metallurgy and engineering were only in their infancy during the [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). Some of his smaller inventions, however, entered the world of manufacturing unheralded, such as an automated bobbin winder and a machine for testing the tensile strength of wire. He made substantial discoveries in [anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy"), civil engineering, [hydrodynamics](/wiki/Fluid_dynamics "Fluid dynamics"), geology, [optics](/wiki/Optics "Optics"), and [tribology](/wiki/Tribology "Tribology"), but he did not publish his findings and they had little to no direct influence on subsequent science.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECapra20075–6-12)

## Biography

### Early life (1452–1472)

#### Birth and background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Baptism_record_of_Leonardo_Da_Vinci.jpg/250px-Baptism_record_of_Leonardo_Da_Vinci.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baptism_record_of_Leonardo_Da_Vinci.jpg)

Leonardo da Vinci's baptism record

Leonardo da Vinci, properly named Leonardo di ser Piero da Vinci[[b]](#cite_note-IPA-4) ("Leonardo, son of ser Piero from Vinci"),[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19987-13)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp20061-14)[[d]](#cite_note-ser-15) was born on 15 April 1452 in, or close to, the [Tuscan](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany") hill town of [Vinci](/wiki/Vinci,_Tuscany "Vinci, Tuscany"), Italy, 20 miles from [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19985-16)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage17_17]-17)[[e]](#cite_note-21) He was born [out of wedlock](/wiki/Legitimacy_(family_law) "Legitimacy (family law)") to Piero da Vinci (Ser Piero da Vinci d'Antonio di ser Piero di ser Guido; 1426–1504),[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBambach201916,_24-22) a Florentine [legal notary](/wiki/Civil_law_notary "Civil law notary"),[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19985-16) and [Caterina di Meo Lippi](/wiki/Caterina_di_Meo_Lippi "Caterina di Meo Lippi") (c. 1434–1494), from the lower class.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarani200313-23)[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBambach201916-24)[[f]](#cite_note-27) It remains uncertain where Leonardo was born; the traditional account, from a local oral tradition recorded by the historian [Emanuele Repetti](/wiki/Emanuele_Repetti "Emanuele Repetti"),[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBambach201924-28) is that he was born in [Anchiano](/wiki/Anchiano "Anchiano"), a country hamlet that would have offered sufficient privacy for the illegitimate birth, though it is still possible he was born in a house in Florence that Ser Piero almost certainly had.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage18_18]-29)[[a]](#cite_note-Birthplace-3) Leonardo's parents both married separately the year after his birth. Caterina – who later appears in Leonardo's notes as only "Caterina" or "Catelina" – is usually identified as the Caterina Buti del Vacca, who married the local artisan Antonio di Piero Buti del Vacca, nicknamed *L'Accattabriga*, 'the quarrelsome one'.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarani200313-23)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBambach201924-28) Having been betrothed to her the previous year, Ser Piero married Albiera Amadori and after her death in 1464, went on to have three subsequent marriages.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBambach201924-28)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKempPallanti201765-30)[[g]](#cite_note-31) From all the marriages, Leonardo eventually had 16 half-siblings (of whom 11 survived infancy)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKempPallanti201765–66-32) who were much younger than he (the last was born when Leonardo was 46 years old)[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKempPallanti201765–66-32) and with whom he had very little contact.[[h]](#cite_note-35)

[![Photo of a building of rough stone with small windows, surrounded by olive trees](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Geburtshaus_von_Leonardo_da_Vinci_in_Vinci_%28Toskana%29.jpg/250px-Geburtshaus_von_Leonardo_da_Vinci_in_Vinci_%28Toskana%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geburtshaus_von_Leonardo_da_Vinci_in_Vinci_(Toskana).jpg)

The possible birthplace and childhood home of Leonardo in [Anchiano](/wiki/Anchiano "Anchiano"), [Vinci](/wiki/Vinci,_Tuscany "Vinci, Tuscany"), Italy

Very little is known about Leonardo's childhood and much is shrouded in myth, partially because of his biography in the frequently apocryphal *[Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects](/wiki/Lives_of_the_Most_Excellent_Painters,_Sculptors,_and_Architects "Lives of the Most Excellent Painters, Sculptors, and Architects")* (1550) by 16th-century art historian [Giorgio Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19981,_5-36)[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarani200312-37) Tax records indicate that by at least 1457 he lived in the household of his paternal grandfather, Antonio da Vinci,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19985-16) but it is possible that he spent the years before then in the care of his mother in Vinci, either Anchiano or Campo Zeppi in the parish of San Pantaleone.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown1998175-38)[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage28_28]-39) He is thought to have been close to his uncle, Francesco da Vinci,[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2003-6) but his father was probably in Florence most of the time.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19985-16) Ser Piero, who was the descendant of a long line of notaries, established an official residence in Florence by at least 1469 and had a successful career.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19985-16) Despite his family history, Leonardo only received a basic and informal education in (vernacular) writing, reading, and mathematics; possibly because his artistic talents were recognised early, so his family decided to focus their attention there.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrown19985-16)

Later in life, Leonardo recorded his earliest memory, now in the [Codex Atlanticus](/wiki/Codex_Atlanticus "Codex Atlanticus").[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage30_30],_[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage506_506]-40) While writing on the flight of birds, he recalled as an infant when a [kite](/wiki/Kite_(bird) "Kite (bird)") came to his cradle and opened his mouth with its tail; commentators still debate whether the anecdote was an actual memory or a fantasy.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage30_30]-41)

#### Verrocchio's workshop

[![Painting showing Jesus, naked except for a loin-cloth, standing in a shallow stream in a rocky landscape, while to the right, John the Baptist, identifiable by the cross that he carries, tips water over Jesus' head. Two angels kneel at the left. Above Jesus are the hands of God, and a dove descending](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Andrea_del_Verrocchio%2C_Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Baptism_of_Christ_-_Uffizi.jpg/250px-Andrea_del_Verrocchio%2C_Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Baptism_of_Christ_-_Uffizi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrea_del_Verrocchio,_Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Baptism_of_Christ_-_Uffizi.jpg)

*[The Baptism of Christ](/wiki/The_Baptism_of_Christ_(Verrocchio) "The Baptism of Christ (Verrocchio)")* (1472–1475) by [Verrocchio](/wiki/Verrocchio "Verrocchio") and Leonardo, [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi") Gallery

In the mid-1460s, Leonardo's family moved to Florence, which at the time was the centre of Christian [Humanist](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") thought and culture.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci197713-42) Around the age of 14,[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197211-33) he became a *garzone* (studio boy) in the workshop of [Andrea del Verrocchio](/wiki/Andrea_del_Verrocchio "Andrea del Verrocchio"), who was the leading Florentine painter and sculptor of his time.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci197713-42) This was about the time of the death of Verrocchio's master, the great sculptor [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello").[[i]](#cite_note-44) Leonardo became an apprentice by the age of 17 and remained in training for seven years.[[36]](#cite_note-45) Other famous painters apprenticed in the workshop or associated with it include [Ghirlandaio](/wiki/Domenico_Ghirlandaio "Domenico Ghirlandaio"), [Perugino](/wiki/Perugino "Perugino"), [Botticelli](/wiki/Botticelli "Botticelli"), and [Lorenzo di Credi](/wiki/Lorenzo_di_Credi "Lorenzo di Credi").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) Leonardo was exposed to both theoretical training and a wide range of technical skills,[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci197727-48) including drafting, chemistry, metallurgy, metal working, plaster casting, leather working, mechanics, and woodwork, as well as the artistic skills of drawing, painting, sculpting, and modelling.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMartindale1972-49)[[j]](#cite_note-50)

Leonardo was a contemporary of Botticelli, Ghirlandaio and Perugino, who were all slightly older than he was.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci19779–20-51) He would have met them at the workshop of Verrocchio or at the [Platonic Academy](/wiki/Platonic_Academy_(Florence) "Platonic Academy (Florence)") of the [Medici](/wiki/Medici "Medici").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46) Florence was ornamented by the works of artists such as Donatello's contemporaries [Masaccio](/wiki/Masaccio "Masaccio"), whose figurative [frescoes](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") were imbued with realism and emotion, and [Ghiberti](/wiki/Lorenzo_Ghiberti "Lorenzo Ghiberti"), whose *[Gates of Paradise](/wiki/Florence_Baptistery#Lorenzo_Ghiberti "Florence Baptistery")*, gleaming with [gold leaf](/wiki/Gold_leaf "Gold leaf"), displayed the art of combining complex figure compositions with detailed architectural backgrounds. [Piero della Francesca](/wiki/Piero_della_Francesca "Piero della Francesca") had made a detailed study of [perspective](/wiki/Perspective_(graphical) "Perspective (graphical)"),[[42]](#cite_note-52) and was the first painter to make a scientific study of light. These studies and [Leon Battista Alberti](/wiki/Leon_Battista_Alberti "Leon Battista Alberti")'s treatise *[De pictura](/wiki/De_pictura "De pictura")* were to have a profound effect on younger artists and in particular on Leonardo's own observations and artworks.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartt1970127–133-43)[[43]](#cite_note-Rach-53)

Much of the painting in Verrocchio's workshop was done by his assistants. According to Vasari, Leonardo collaborated with Verrocchio on his *[The Baptism of Christ](/wiki/The_Baptism_of_Christ_(Verrocchio_and_Leonardo) "The Baptism of Christ (Verrocchio and Leonardo)")* (c. 1472–1475), painting the young angel holding Jesus's robe with skill so far superior to his master's that Verrocchio purportedly put down his brush and never painted again[[‡ 1]](#cite_note-54) (the latter claim probably being apocryphal).[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196783-19) The new technique of [oil paint](/wiki/Oil_painting "Oil painting") was applied to areas of the mostly [tempera](/wiki/Tempera "Tempera") work, including the landscape, the rocks seen through the brown mountain stream, and much of Jesus's figure, indicating Leonardo's hand.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196788-55) Additionally, Leonardo may have been a model for two works by Verrocchio: the bronze statue of *[David](/wiki/David_(Verrocchio) "David (Verrocchio)")* in the [Bargello](/wiki/Bargello "Bargello") and the [archangel Raphael](/wiki/Archangel_Raphael "Archangel Raphael") in *[Tobias and the Angel](/wiki/Tobias_and_the_Angel_(Verrocchio) "Tobias and the Angel (Verrocchio)")*.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196783-19)

Vasari tells a story of Leonardo as a very young man: a local peasant made himself a round [buckler](/wiki/Buckler "Buckler") shield and requested that Ser Piero have it painted for him. Leonardo, inspired by the story of [Medusa](/wiki/Medusa "Medusa"), responded with a [painting of a monster](/wiki/Medusa_(Leonardo) "Medusa (Leonardo)") spitting fire that was so terrifying that his father bought a different shield to give to the peasant and sold Leonardo's to a Florentine art dealer for 100 [ducats](/wiki/Ducat "Ducat"), who in turn sold it to the [Duke of Milan](/wiki/List_of_dukes_of_Milan "List of dukes of Milan").[[‡ 2]](#cite_note-56)

### First Florentine period (1472 – c. 1482)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Adoration_of_the_Magi_%28Leonardo%29.jpg/250px-Adoration_of_the_Magi_%28Leonardo%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adoration_of_the_Magi_(Leonardo).jpg)

*Adoration of the Magi* c. 1478–1482,[[d 1]](#cite_note-57) [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi"), Florence

By 1472, at the age of 20, Leonardo qualified as a master in the [Guild of Saint Luke](/wiki/Guild_of_Saint_Luke "Guild of Saint Luke"), the guild of artists and doctors of medicine,[[k]](#cite_note-58) but even after his father set him up in his own workshop, his attachment to Verrocchio was such that he continued to collaborate and live with him.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197213-59) Leonardo's earliest known dated work is a 1473 pen-and-ink drawing of the [Arno](/wiki/Arno "Arno") valley (see below).[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)[[46]](#cite_note-Polidoro-60)[[l]](#cite_note-61) According to Vasari, the young Leonardo was the first to suggest making the Arno river a navigable channel between Florence and [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa").[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197215-62)

In January 1478, Leonardo received an independent commission to paint an altarpiece for the Chapel of Saint Bernard in the Florentine town hall, the [Palazzo della Signoria](/wiki/Palazzo_Vecchio "Palazzo Vecchio"),[[48]](#cite_note-63) an indication of his independence from Verrocchio's studio. An anonymous early biographer, known as [Anonimo Gaddiano](/wiki/Anonimo_Gaddiano "Anonimo Gaddiano"), claims that in 1480 Leonardo was living with the Medici and often worked in the garden of the [Piazza San Marco, Florence](/wiki/Piazza_San_Marco,_Florence "Piazza San Marco, Florence"), where a [Neoplatonic academy](/wiki/Platonic_Academy_(Florence) "Platonic Academy (Florence)") of artists, poets and philosophers organised by the Medici met.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196783-19)[[m]](#cite_note-64) In March 1481, he received a commission from the monks of [San Donato in Scopeto](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chiesa_di_San_Donato_in_Scopeto "it:Chiesa di San Donato in Scopeto") for *[The Adoration of the Magi](/wiki/Adoration_of_the_Magi_(Leonardo) "Adoration of the Magi (Leonardo)")*.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman197577–78-65) Neither of these initial commissions were completed, being abandoned when Leonardo went to offer his services to [Duke of Milan](/wiki/Duke_of_Milan "Duke of Milan") [Ludovico Sforza](/wiki/Ludovico_Sforza "Ludovico Sforza"). Leonardo wrote Sforza [a letter](/wiki/Letter_from_Leonardo_da_Vinci_to_Ludovico_Sforza "Letter from Leonardo da Vinci to Ludovico Sforza") which described the diverse things that he could achieve in the fields of engineering and weapon design, and mentioned that he could paint.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197253–54-66) He brought with him a silver string instrument – either a [lute](/wiki/Lute "Lute") or [lyre](/wiki/Lyre "Lyre") – in the form of a horse's head.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197253–54-66)

With Alberti, Leonardo visited the home of the Medici and through them came to know the older Humanist philosophers of whom [Marsiglio Ficino](/wiki/Marsiglio_Ficino "Marsiglio Ficino"), proponent of [Neoplatonism](/wiki/Neoplatonism "Neoplatonism"); [Cristoforo Landino](/wiki/Cristoforo_Landino "Cristoforo Landino"), writer of commentaries on Classical writings, and [John Argyropoulos](/wiki/John_Argyropoulos "John Argyropoulos"), teacher of Greek and translator of [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") were the foremost. Also associated with the Platonic Academy of the Medici was Leonardo's contemporary, the brilliant young poet and philosopher [Pico della Mirandola](/wiki/Pico_della_Mirandola "Pico della Mirandola").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci19779–20-51)[[43]](#cite_note-Rach-53)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson1974-67) In 1482, Leonardo was sent as an ambassador by [Lorenzo de' Medici](/wiki/Lorenzo_de%27_Medici "Lorenzo de' Medici") to [Ludovico il Moro](/wiki/Ludovico_il_Moro "Ludovico il Moro"), who ruled [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") between 1479 and 1499.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci19779–20-51)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196783-19)

### First Milanese period (c. 1482–1499)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_Vergine_delle_Rocce_%28Louvre%29.jpg/250px-Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_Vergine_delle_Rocce_%28Louvre%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_Vergine_delle_Rocce_(Louvre).jpg)

*[Virgin of the Rocks](/wiki/Virgin_of_the_Rocks "Virgin of the Rocks")*, c. 1483–1493,[[d 2]](#cite_note-68) [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre") version

Leonardo worked in Milan from 1482 until 1499. He was commissioned to paint the *[Virgin of the Rocks](/wiki/Virgin_of_the_Rocks "Virgin of the Rocks")* for the [Confraternity of the Immaculate Conception](/wiki/Confraternity_of_the_Immaculate_Conception "Confraternity of the Immaculate Conception") and *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo_da_Vinci) "The Last Supper (Leonardo da Vinci)")* for the monastery of [Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_(Milan) "Santa Maria delle Grazie (Milan)").[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2011-69) In the spring of 1485, Leonardo travelled to [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary_(1000%E2%80%931538) "Kingdom of Hungary (1000–1538)") (on behalf of Sforza) to meet king [Matthias Corvinus](/wiki/Matthias_Corvinus "Matthias Corvinus"), and was commissioned by him to paint a [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(art) "Madonna (art)").[[53]](#cite_note-70) In 1490 he was called as a consultant, together with [Francesco di Giorgio Martini](/wiki/Francesco_di_Giorgio_Martini "Francesco di Giorgio Martini"), for the building site of the [cathedral](/wiki/Pavia_Cathedral "Pavia Cathedral") of [Pavia](/wiki/Pavia "Pavia")[[54]](#cite_note-71)[[55]](#cite_note-72) and was struck by the equestrian statue of [Regisole](/wiki/Regisole "Regisole"), of which he left a sketch.[[56]](#cite_note-73) Leonardo was employed on many other projects for Sforza, such as preparation of floats and pageants for special occasions; [a drawing of](/wiki/File:Study_for_the_Cupola_of_Milan_Cathedral_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci.jpg "File:Study for the Cupola of Milan Cathedral by Leonardo da Vinci.jpg"), and wooden model for, a competition to design the [cupola](/wiki/Cupola "Cupola") for [Milan Cathedral](/wiki/Milan_Cathedral "Milan Cathedral");[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197279-74) and a model for a huge [equestrian monument](/wiki/Equestrian_monument "Equestrian monument") to Ludovico's predecessor [Francesco Sforza](/wiki/Francesco_Sforza "Francesco Sforza"). This would have surpassed in size the only two large equestrian statues of the Renaissance, [Donatello](/wiki/Donatello "Donatello")'s *[Gattamelata](/wiki/Equestrian_statue_of_Gattamelata "Equestrian statue of Gattamelata")* in Padua and Verrocchio's *[Bartolomeo Colleoni](/wiki/Bartolomeo_Colleoni "Bartolomeo Colleoni")* in Venice, and became known as the *[Gran Cavallo](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_horse "Leonardo's horse")*.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) Leonardo completed a model for the horse and made detailed plans for its [casting](/wiki/Casting_(metalworking) "Casting (metalworking)"),[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) but in November 1494, Ludovico gave the metal to [his brother-in-law](/wiki/Ercole_I_d%27Este,_Duke_of_Ferrara "Ercole I d'Este, Duke of Ferrara") to be used for a cannon to defend the city from [Charles VIII of France](/wiki/Charles_VIII_of_France "Charles VIII of France").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)

Contemporary correspondence records that Leonardo and his assistants were commissioned by the Duke of Milan to paint the [Sala delle Asse](/wiki/Sala_delle_Asse "Sala delle Asse") in the [Sforza Castle](/wiki/Sforza_Castle "Sforza Castle"), c. 1498.[[58]](#cite_note-75) The project became a [trompe-l'œil](/wiki/Trompe-l%27%C5%93il "Trompe-l'œil") decoration that made the great hall appear to be a pergola created by the interwoven limbs of sixteen mulberry trees,[[59]](#cite_note-76) whose canopy included an intricate labyrinth of leaves and knots on the ceiling.[[60]](#cite_note-77)

### Second Florentine period (1500–1508)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Virgin_and_Child_with_Ss_Anne_and_John_the_Baptist.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Virgin_and_Child_with_Ss_Anne_and_John_the_Baptist.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Virgin_and_Child_with_Ss_Anne_and_John_the_Baptist.jpg)

*[The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne and Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/The_Virgin_and_Child_with_Saint_Anne_and_Saint_John_the_Baptist "The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne and Saint John the Baptist")*, c. 1499–1508, [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery"), London

When Ludovico Sforza was [overthrown by France](/wiki/Battle_of_Novara_(1500) "Battle of Novara (1500)") in 1500, Leonardo fled Milan for [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"), accompanied by his assistant [Salaì](/wiki/Sala%C3%AC "Salaì") and friend, the mathematician [Luca Pacioli](/wiki/Luca_Pacioli "Luca Pacioli").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196785-79) In Venice, Leonardo was employed as a military architect and engineer, devising methods to defend the city from naval attack.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46) On his return to Florence in 1500, he and his household were guests of the Servite monks at the monastery of [Santissima Annunziata](/wiki/Santissima_Annunziata,_Florence "Santissima Annunziata, Florence") and were provided with a workshop where, according to Vasari, Leonardo created the [cartoon](/wiki/The_Virgin_and_Child_with_Saint_Anne_(cartoon) "The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne (cartoon)") of *[The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne and Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/The_Virgin_and_Child_with_Saint_Anne_and_Saint_John_the_Baptist "The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne and Saint John the Baptist")*, a work that won such admiration that "men [and] women, young and old" flocked to see it "as if they were going to a solemn festival."[[‡ 3]](#cite_note-V265-80)[[n]](#cite_note-82)

In [Cesena](/wiki/Cesena "Cesena") in 1502, Leonardo entered the service of [Cesare Borgia](/wiki/Cesare_Borgia "Cesare Borgia"), the son of [Pope Alexander VI](/wiki/Pope_Alexander_VI "Pope Alexander VI"), acting as a military architect and engineer and travelling throughout Italy with his patron.[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196785-79) Leonardo created a map of Cesare Borgia's stronghold, a town plan of [Imola](/wiki/Imola "Imola") in order to win his patronage. Upon seeing it, Cesare hired Leonardo as his chief military engineer and architect. Later in the year, Leonardo produced another map for his patron, one of [Chiana Valley](/wiki/Chiana_Valley "Chiana Valley"), Tuscany, so as to give his patron a better overlay of the land and greater strategic position. He created this map in conjunction with his other project of constructing a dam from the sea to Florence, in order to allow a supply of water to sustain the canal during all seasons.

Leonardo had left Borgia's service and returned to Florence by early 1503,[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972124-83) where he rejoined the [Guild of Saint Luke](/wiki/Guild_of_Saint_Luke#Italy "Guild of Saint Luke") on 18 October of that year. By this same month, Leonardo had begun working on a portrait of [Lisa del Giocondo](/wiki/Lisa_del_Giocondo "Lisa del Giocondo"), the model for the *[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")*,[[65]](#cite_note-84)[[66]](#cite_note-85) which he would continue working on until his later years. In January 1504, he was part of a committee formed to recommend where Michelangelo's statue of *[David](/wiki/David_(Michelangelo) "David (Michelangelo)")* should be placed.[[67]](#cite_note-86) He then spent two years in Florence designing and painting a mural of *[The Battle of Anghiari](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Anghiari_(painting) "The Battle of Anghiari (painting)")* for the Signoria,[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196785-79) with Michelangelo designing its companion piece, *[The Battle of Cascina](/wiki/Battle_of_Cascina_(Michelangelo) "Battle of Cascina (Michelangelo)")*.[[o]](#cite_note-89)

In 1506, Leonardo was summoned to Milan by [Charles II d'Amboise](/wiki/Charles_II_d%27Amboise "Charles II d'Amboise"), the acting [French governor](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") of the city.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972145-90) There, Leonardo took on another pupil, Count [Francesco Melzi](/wiki/Francesco_Melzi "Francesco Melzi"), the son of a [Lombard](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") aristocrat, who is considered to have been his favourite student.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46) The [Council of Florence](/wiki/Council_of_Florence "Council of Florence") wished Leonardo to return promptly to finish *The Battle of Anghiari*, but he was given leave at the behest of [Louis XII](/wiki/Louis_XII "Louis XII"), who considered commissioning the artist to make some portraits.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972145-90) Leonardo may have commenced a project for an equestrian figure of d'Amboise;[[71]](#cite_note-91) [a wax model attributed to him](/wiki/Horse_and_Rider_(wax_sculpture) "Horse and Rider (wax sculpture)") survives and would be the only extant example of Leonardo's sculpture, but the [attribution is not widely accepted](/wiki/Horse_and_Rider_(wax_sculpture)#Attribution_and_disputes "Horse and Rider (wax sculpture)"). Leonardo was otherwise free to pursue his scientific interests.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972145-90) Many of Leonardo's most prominent pupils either knew or worked with him in Milan,[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46) including [Bernardino Luini](/wiki/Bernardino_Luini "Bernardino Luini"), [Giovanni Antonio Boltraffio](/wiki/Giovanni_Antonio_Boltraffio "Giovanni Antonio Boltraffio"), and [Marco d'Oggiono](/wiki/Marco_d%27Oggiono "Marco d'Oggiono"). In 1507, Leonardo was in Florence sorting out a dispute with his brothers over the estate of his father, who had died in 1504.
