### Second Milanese period (1508–1513)

By 1508, Leonardo was back in Milan, living in his own house in Porta Orientale in the parish of Santa Babila.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196786-92)

In 1512, Leonardo was working on plans for an equestrian monument for [Gian Giacomo Trivulzio](/wiki/Gian_Giacomo_Trivulzio "Gian Giacomo Trivulzio"), but this was prevented by an invasion of a confederation of Swiss, Spanish and Venetian forces, which drove the French from Milan. Leonardo stayed in the city, spending several months in 1513 at the Medici's [Vaprio d'Adda](/wiki/Vaprio_d%27Adda "Vaprio d'Adda") villa.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972149–150-93)

### Rome and France (1513–1519)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_A_deluge_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_A_deluge_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_A_deluge_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

An apocalyptic deluge drawn in black chalk by Leonardo near the end of his life (part of a series of 10, paired with written description in his notebooks)[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972151-94)

In March 1513, Lorenzo de' Medici's son [Giovanni](/wiki/Pope_Leo_X "Pope Leo X") assumed the papacy (as Leo X); Leonardo went to Rome that September, where he was received by the pope's brother [Giuliano](/wiki/Giuliano_de%27_Medici,_Duke_of_Nemours "Giuliano de' Medici, Duke of Nemours").[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972149–150-93) From September 1513 to 1516, Leonardo spent much of his time living in the [Belvedere Courtyard](/wiki/Belvedere_Courtyard "Belvedere Courtyard") in the [Apostolic Palace](/wiki/Apostolic_Palace "Apostolic Palace"), where Michelangelo and [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael") were both active.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196786-92) Leonardo was given an allowance of 33 ducats a month and, according to Vasari, decorated a lizard with scales dipped in [quicksilver](/wiki/Mercury_(element) "Mercury (element)").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972150-95) The pope gave him a painting commission of unknown subject matter, but cancelled it when the artist set about developing a new kind of [varnish](/wiki/Varnish "Varnish").[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972150-95)[[p]](#cite_note-96) Leonardo became ill, in what may have been the first of multiple [strokes](/wiki/Stroke "Stroke") leading to his death.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972150-95) He practised botany in the [Vatican Gardens](/wiki/Vatican_Gardens "Vatican Gardens"), and was commissioned to make plans for the Pope's proposed draining of the [Pontine Marshes](/wiki/Pontine_Marshes "Pontine Marshes").[[76]](#cite_note-97) He also dissected [cadavers](/wiki/Cadaver "Cadaver"), making notes for a treatise on [vocal cords](/wiki/Vocal_cords "Vocal cords");[[77]](#cite_note-98) these he gave to an official in hopes of regaining the Pope's favour, but he was unsuccessful.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972150-95)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/The_castle_of_Amboise.jpg/250px-The_castle_of_Amboise.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_castle_of_Amboise.jpg)

Drawing of the [Château d'Amboise](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_d%27Amboise "Château d'Amboise") (c. 1518) attributed to [Francesco Melzi](/wiki/Francesco_Melzi "Francesco Melzi")

In October 1515, King [Francis I of France](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France") recaptured Milan.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman197577–78-65)[[q]](#cite_note-100) On 21 March 1516 Antonio Maria Pallavicini, the French ambassador to the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), received a letter sent from [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") a week previously by the royal advisor [Guillaume Gouffier, seigneur de Bonnivet](/wiki/Guillaume_Gouffier,_seigneur_de_Bonnivet "Guillaume Gouffier, seigneur de Bonnivet"), containing the French king's instructions to assist Leonardo in his relocation to France and to inform the artist that the King was eagerly awaiting his arrival. Pallavicini was also asked to reassure Leonardo that he would be well received at court, both by the King and by his mother, [Louise of Savoy](/wiki/Louise_of_Savoy "Louise of Savoy").[[79]](#cite_note-101) Leonardo entered Francis's service later that year, and was given the use of the manor house [Clos Lucé](/wiki/Clos_Luc%C3%A9 "Clos Lucé") near the King's residence at the royal [Château d'Amboise](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_d%27Amboise "Château d'Amboise"). He was frequently visited by Francis, and drew plans for an immense [castle town](/wiki/Castle_town "Castle town") the King intended to erect at [Romorantin](/wiki/Romorantin-Lanthenay "Romorantin-Lanthenay"). He also made a mechanical lion, which during a pageant walked towards the King and – upon being struck by a wand – opened its chest to reveal a cluster of lilies.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972163,_164-102)[[‡ 3]](#cite_note-V265-80)[[r]](#cite_note-104)

Leonardo was accompanied during this time by his friend and apprentice Francesco Melzi, and was supported by a pension totalling 10,000 [scudi](/wiki/Scudi "Scudi").[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196786-92) At some point, Melzi drew a [portrait of Leonardo](/wiki/File:A_portrait_of_Leonardo,_by_Francesco_Melzi.jpg "File:A portrait of Leonardo, by Francesco Melzi.jpg"); the [only others known](/wiki/Portraits_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Portraits of Leonardo da Vinci") from his lifetime were a sketch by an unknown assistant on the back of one of Leonardo's studies (c. 1517)[[82]](#cite_note-105) and a drawing by [Giovanni Ambrogio Figino](/wiki/Giovanni_Ambrogio_Figino "Giovanni Ambrogio Figino") depicting an elderly Leonardo with his right arm wrapped in clothing.[[83]](#cite_note-paralysis-106)[[s]](#cite_note-108) The latter, in addition to the record of an October 1517 visit by [Louis d'Aragon](/wiki/Luigi_d%27Aragona "Luigi d'Aragona"),[[t]](#cite_note-110) confirms an account of Leonardo's right hand being paralytic when he was 65,[[86]](#cite_note-seeker-111) which may indicate why he left works such as the *Mona Lisa* unfinished.[[84]](#cite_note-guardian2005-107)[[87]](#cite_note-112)[[88]](#cite_note-lazzeri-113) He continued to work at some capacity until eventually becoming ill and bedridden for several months.[[86]](#cite_note-seeker-111)

### Death

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Francois_Ier_Leonard_de_Vinci-Jean_Auguste_Dominique_Ingres.jpg/250px-Francois_Ier_Leonard_de_Vinci-Jean_Auguste_Dominique_Ingres.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francois_Ier_Leonard_de_Vinci-Jean_Auguste_Dominique_Ingres.jpg)

*[The Death of Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/The_Death_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci "The Death of Leonardo da Vinci")*, by [Ingres](/wiki/Jean-Auguste-Dominique_Ingres "Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres"), 1818[[u]](#cite_note-edict-114)

Leonardo died at Clos Lucé on 2 May 1519 at the age of 67, possibly of a stroke.[[89]](#cite_note-neurology-115)[[88]](#cite_note-lazzeri-113)[[90]](#cite_note-116) Francis I had become a close friend. Vasari describes Leonardo as lamenting on his deathbed, full of repentance, that "he had offended against God and men by failing to practice his art as he should have done."[[91]](#cite_note-117) Vasari states that in his last days, Leonardo sent for a priest to make his confession and to receive the [Holy Sacrament](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist").[[‡ 4]](#cite_note-118) Vasari also records that the King held Leonardo's head in his arms as he died, although this story may be legend rather than fact.[[v]](#cite_note-119)[[u]](#cite_note-edict-114) In accordance with his will, sixty beggars carrying tapers followed Leonardo's casket.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson1974-67)[[w]](#cite_note-121) Melzi was the principal heir and executor, receiving, as well as money, Leonardo's paintings, tools, library and personal effects. Leonardo's other long-time pupil and companion, Salaì, and his servant Baptista de Vilanis, each received half of Leonardo's [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard").[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp201126-122) His brothers received land, and his serving woman received a fur-lined cloak. On 12 August 1519, [Leonardo's remains](#Location_of_remains) were interred in the Collegiate Church of Saint Florentin at the Château d'Amboise.[[94]](#cite_note-Florentine-123)

Some 20 years after Leonardo's death, Francis was reported by the goldsmith and sculptor [Benvenuto Cellini](/wiki/Benvenuto_Cellini "Benvenuto Cellini") as saying: "There had never been another man born in the world who knew as much as Leonardo, not so much about painting, sculpture and architecture, as that he was a very great philosopher."[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGascaNicolòLucertini2004[httpsarchiveorgdetailsspringer_101007-978-3-0348-7951-4pagen28_13]-124)

At the time of his death in 1524, Salaì owned a painting referred to as *Joconda* in a posthumous inventory of his belongings; it was assessed at 505 lire, an exceptionally high valuation for a small panel portrait.[[96]](#cite_note-NR-125)

## Personal life

Main article: [Personal life of Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Personal_life_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Personal life of Leonardo da Vinci")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Saint_John_the_Baptist_C2RMF_retouched.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Saint_John_the_Baptist_C2RMF_retouched.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Saint_John_the_Baptist_C2RMF_retouched.jpg)

*[Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/Saint_John_the_Baptist_(Leonardo) "Saint John the Baptist (Leonardo)")* c. 1507–1516,[[d 3]](#cite_note-126) Louvre. Leonardo is thought to have used [Salaì](/wiki/Sala%C3%AC "Salaì") as the model.[[97]](#cite_note-Pedretti-2009-127)

Despite the thousands of pages Leonardo left in notebooks and manuscripts, he scarcely made reference to his personal life.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZöllner201920-2)

Within Leonardo's lifetime, his extraordinary powers of invention, his "great physical beauty" and "infinite grace," as described by [Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari"),[[‡ 5]](#cite_note-128) as well as all other aspects of his life, attracted the curiosity of others. One such aspect was his love for animals, likely including [vegetarianism](/wiki/Vegetarianism "Vegetarianism") and according to Vasari, a habit of purchasing caged birds and releasing them.[[98]](#cite_note-129)[[‡ 6]](#cite_note-130)

Leonardo had many friends who are now notable either in their fields or for their historical significance, including mathematician [Luca Pacioli](/wiki/Luca_Pacioli "Luca Pacioli"),[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBambach2003-131) with whom he collaborated on the book *[Divina proportione](/wiki/Divina_proportione "Divina proportione")* in the 1490s. Leonardo appears to have had no close relationships with women except for his friendship with [Cecilia Gallerani](/wiki/Cecilia_Gallerani "Cecilia Gallerani") and the two Este sisters, [Beatrice](/wiki/Beatrice_d%27Este "Beatrice d'Este") and [Isabella](/wiki/Isabella_d%27Este "Isabella d'Este").[[100]](#cite_note-132) While on a journey that took him through [Mantua](/wiki/Mantua "Mantua"), he drew a portrait of Isabella that appears to have been used to create a painted portrait, now lost.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46)

[Salaì](/wiki/Sala%C3%AC "Salaì"), or Il Salaino ("The Little Unclean One", i.e., the devil), entered Leonardo's household in 1490 as an assistant. After only a year, Leonardo made a list of his misdemeanours, calling him "a thief, a liar, stubborn, and a glutton," after he had made off with money and valuables on at least five occasions and spent a fortune on clothes.[[101]](#cite_note-133) Nevertheless, Leonardo treated him with great indulgence, and he remained in Leonardo's household for the next thirty years.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196784-134) Salaì executed paintings under the name of Andrea Salaì, but although Vasari claims that Leonardo "taught him many things about painting,"[*[This quote needs a citation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Inline_citation#When_you_must_use_inline_citations "Wikipedia:Inline citation")*] his work is generally considered to be of less artistic merit than others among Leonardo's pupils, such as [Marco d'Oggiono](/wiki/Marco_d%27Oggiono "Marco d'Oggiono") and [Boltraffio](/wiki/Giovanni_Antonio_Boltraffio "Giovanni Antonio Boltraffio").

Beyond friendship, Leonardo kept his private life secret. His sexuality has been the subject of satire, analysis, and speculation. This trend began in the mid-16th century and was revived in the 19th and 20th centuries, most notably by [Sigmund Freud](/wiki/Sigmund_Freud "Sigmund Freud") in his *[Leonardo da Vinci, A Memory of His Childhood](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci,_A_Memory_of_His_Childhood "Leonardo da Vinci, A Memory of His Childhood")*.[[103]](#cite_note-135) Leonardo's most intimate relationships were perhaps with his pupils [Salaì](/wiki/Sala%C3%AC "Salaì") and [Melzi](/wiki/Francesco_Melzi "Francesco Melzi"). Melzi, writing to inform Leonardo's brothers of his death, described Leonardo's feelings for his pupils as both loving and passionate. It has been claimed since the 16th century that these relationships were of a sexual or erotic nature. [Walter Isaacson](/wiki/Walter_Isaacson "Walter Isaacson") in [his biography of Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci_(Isaacson_book) "Leonardo da Vinci (Isaacson book)") makes explicit his opinion that the relations with Salaì were intimate and homosexual.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaacson2017129–138-136)

In 1476, when he was aged twenty-four, court records show that Leonardo and three other young men were charged with [sodomy](/wiki/Sodomy "Sodomy") in an incident involving a known male prostitute. The charges were dismissed for lack of evidence, and there is speculation that since one of the accused, Lionardo de Tornabuoni, was related to Lorenzo de' Medici, the family exerted its influence to secure the dismissal.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaacson2017-137) Since that date much has been written about his presumed homosexuality[[106]](#cite_note-138) and its role in his art, particularly in the [androgyny](/wiki/Androgyny "Androgyny") and [eroticism](/wiki/Eroticism "Eroticism") manifested in *[Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/Saint_John_the_Baptist_(Leonardo) "Saint John the Baptist (Leonardo)")* and *[Bacchus](/wiki/Bacchus_(Leonardo) "Bacchus (Leonardo)")* and more explicitly in erotic drawings.[[107]](#cite_note-139)[[97]](#cite_note-Pedretti-2009-127)[[x]](#cite_note-141)

## Paintings

See also: [List of works by Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/List_of_works_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci "List of works by Leonardo da Vinci")

Despite the recent awareness and admiration of Leonardo as a scientist and inventor, for the better part of four hundred years his fame rested on his achievements as a painter. A handful of works that are either authenticated or attributed to him have been regarded as among the great masterpieces. These paintings are famous for a variety of qualities that have been much imitated by students and discussed at great length by connoisseurs and critics. By the 1490s Leonardo had already been described as a "Divine" painter.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse199811–15-142)

Among the qualities that make Leonardo's work unique are his innovative techniques for laying on the paint; his detailed knowledge of anatomy, light, botany and geology; his interest in [physiognomy](/wiki/Physiognomy "Physiognomy") and the way humans register emotion in expression and gesture; his innovative use of the human form in figurative composition; and his use of subtle gradation of tone. All these qualities come together in his most famous painted works, the *Mona Lisa*, the *Last Supper*, and the *Virgin of the Rocks*.[[y]](#cite_note-143)

### Early works

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Annunciation_%28Leonardo_c._1472%E2%80%931476%29.jpg/250px-Annunciation_%28Leonardo_c._1472%E2%80%931476%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Annunciation_(Leonardo_c._1472%E2%80%931476).jpg)

*[Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation_(Leonardo) "Annunciation (Leonardo)")* c. 1472–1476,[[d 4]](#cite_note-144) [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi"), is thought to be Leonardo's earliest extant and complete major work.

Leonardo first gained attention for his work on the *[Baptism of Christ](/wiki/The_Baptism_of_Christ_(Verrocchio) "The Baptism of Christ (Verrocchio)")*, painted in conjunction with Verrocchio. Two other paintings appear to date from his time at Verrocchio's workshop, both of which are [Annunciations](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation"). One is small, 59 centimetres (23 in) long and 14 cm (5.5 in) high. It is a "[predella](/wiki/Predella "Predella")" to go at the base of a larger composition, a painting by Lorenzo di Credi from which it has become separated. The other is a much larger work, 217 cm (85 in) long.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196788,_90-145) In both Annunciations, Leonardo used a formal arrangement, like two well-known pictures by [Fra Angelico](/wiki/Fra_Angelico "Fra Angelico") of the same subject, of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") sitting or kneeling to the right of the picture, approached from the left by an angel in profile, with a rich flowing garment, raised wings and bearing a lily. Although previously attributed to Ghirlandaio, the larger work is now generally attributed to Leonardo.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarani2003338-146)

In the smaller painting, Mary averts her eyes and folds her hands in a gesture that symbolised submission to God's will. Mary is not submissive, however, in the larger piece. The girl, interrupted in her reading by this unexpected messenger, puts a finger in her bible to mark the place and raises her hand in a formal gesture of greeting or surprise.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartt1970127–133-43) This calm young woman appears to accept her role as the [Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God"), not with resignation but with confidence. In this painting, the young Leonardo presents the humanist face of the Virgin Mary, recognising humanity's role in God's incarnation.

### Paintings of the 1480s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Leonardo_da_Vinci_%E2%80%94_Jerome.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_%E2%80%94_Jerome.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_%E2%80%94_Jerome.jpg)

*[Saint Jerome in the Wilderness](/wiki/Saint_Jerome_in_the_Wilderness_(Leonardo) "Saint Jerome in the Wilderness (Leonardo)")* (unfinished) c. 1480–1490,[[d 5]](#cite_note-147) [Vatican](/wiki/Apostolic_Palace "Apostolic Palace")

In the 1480s, Leonardo received two very important commissions and commenced another work that was of ground-breaking importance in terms of composition. Two of the three were never finished, and the third took so long that it was subject to lengthy negotiations over completion and payment.

One of these paintings was *[Saint Jerome in the Wilderness](/wiki/Saint_Jerome_in_the_Wilderness_(Leonardo) "Saint Jerome in the Wilderness (Leonardo)")*, which Bortolon associates with a difficult period of Leonardo's life, as evidenced in his diary: "I thought I was learning to live; I was only learning to die."[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46) Although the painting is barely begun, the composition can be seen and is very unusual.[[z]](#cite_note-149) [Jerome](/wiki/Jerome "Jerome"), as a [penitent](/wiki/Penitent "Penitent"), occupies the middle of the picture, set on a slight diagonal and viewed somewhat from above. His kneeling form takes on a trapezoid shape, with one arm stretched to the outer edge of the painting and his gaze looking in the opposite direction. J. Wasserman points out the link between this painting and Leonardo's anatomical studies.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman1975104–106-148) Across the foreground sprawls his symbol, a great lion whose body and tail make a double spiral across the base of the picture space. The other remarkable feature is the sketchy landscape of craggy rocks against which the figure is silhouetted.

The daring display of figure composition, the landscape elements and personal drama also appear in the great unfinished masterpiece, the *[Adoration of the Magi](/wiki/Adoration_of_the_Magi_(Leonardo) "Adoration of the Magi (Leonardo)")*, a commission from the Monks of San Donato a Scopeto. It is a complex composition, of about 250 × 250 centimetres. Leonardo did numerous drawings and preparatory studies, including a detailed one in linear perspective of the ruined [classical architecture](/wiki/Classical_architecture "Classical architecture") that forms part of the background. In 1482 Leonardo went to Milan at the behest of Lorenzo de' Medici in order to win favour with Ludovico il Moro, and the painting was abandoned.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196783-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Lady_with_an_Ermine_-_Leonardo_da_Vinci_%28adjusted_levels%29.jpg/250px-Lady_with_an_Ermine_-_Leonardo_da_Vinci_%28adjusted_levels%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lady_with_an_Ermine_-_Leonardo_da_Vinci_(adjusted_levels).jpg)

*[Lady with an Ermine](/wiki/Lady_with_an_Ermine "Lady with an Ermine")*, c. 1489–1491,[[d 6]](#cite_note-150) [Czartoryski Museum](/wiki/Czartoryski_Museum "Czartoryski Museum"), [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), Poland

The third important work of this period is the *[Virgin of the Rocks](/wiki/Virgin_of_the_Rocks "Virgin of the Rocks")*, commissioned in Milan for the Confraternity of the Immaculate Conception. The painting, to be done with the assistance of the [de Predis brothers](/wiki/Giovanni_Ambrogio_de_Predis "Giovanni Ambrogio de Predis"), was to fill a large complex [altarpiece](/wiki/Altarpiece "Altarpiece").[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman1975108-151) Leonardo chose to paint an apocryphal moment of the infancy of Christ when the infant [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"), in protection of an angel, met the Holy Family on the road to Egypt. The painting demonstrates an eerie beauty as the graceful figures kneel in adoration around the infant Christ in a wild landscape of tumbling rock and whirling water.[[114]](#cite_note-152) While the painting is quite large, about 200 × 120 centimetres, it is not nearly as complex as the painting ordered by the monks of San Donato, having only four figures rather than about fifty and a rocky landscape rather than architectural details. The painting was eventually finished; in fact, two versions of the painting were finished: one remained at the chapel of the Confraternity, while Leonardo took the other to France. The Brothers did not get their painting, however, nor the de Predis their payment, until the next century.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196785-79)

Leonardo's most remarkable portrait of this period is the *[Lady with an Ermine](/wiki/Lady_with_an_Ermine "Lady with an Ermine")*, presumed to be [Cecilia Gallerani](/wiki/Cecilia_Gallerani "Cecilia Gallerani") (c. 1483–1490), lover of Ludovico Sforza.[[115]](#cite_note-treasures-153)[[116]](#cite_note-154) The painting is characterised by the pose of the figure with the head turned at a very different angle to the torso, unusual at a date when many portraits were still rigidly in profile. The ermine plainly carries symbolic meaning, relating either to the sitter, or to Ludovico who belonged to the prestigious [Order of the Ermine](/wiki/Order_of_the_Ermine_(France) "Order of the Ermine (France)").[[115]](#cite_note-treasures-153)

### Paintings of the 1490s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/The_Last_Supper_-_Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_High_Resolution_32x16.jpg/500px-The_Last_Supper_-_Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_High_Resolution_32x16.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Last_Supper_-_Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_High_Resolution_32x16.jpg)

*[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")*,[[d 7]](#cite_note-155) [Convent of Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie_(Milan) "Santa Maria delle Grazie (Milan)"), Milan (c. 1492–1498)

Leonardo's most famous painting of the 1490s is *[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")*, commissioned for the refectory of the Convent of Santa Maria della Grazie in Milan. It represents the [last meal](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") shared by Jesus with his disciples before his capture and death, and shows the moment when Jesus has just said "one of you will betray me", and the consternation that this statement caused.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)

The writer [Matteo Bandello](/wiki/Matteo_Bandello "Matteo Bandello") observed Leonardo at work and wrote that some days he would paint from dawn till dusk without stopping to eat and then not paint for three or four days at a time.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman1975124-156) This was beyond the comprehension of the [prior](/wiki/Prior_(ecclesiastical) "Prior (ecclesiastical)") of the convent, who hounded him until Leonardo asked Ludovico to intervene. Vasari describes how Leonardo, troubled over his ability to adequately depict the faces of Christ and the traitor [Judas](/wiki/Judas_Iscariot "Judas Iscariot"), told the duke that he might be obliged to use the prior as his model.[[‡ 7]](#cite_note-:0-157)

The painting was acclaimed as a masterpiece of design and characterisation,[[‡ 8]](#cite_note-158) but it deteriorated rapidly, so that within a hundred years it was described by one viewer as "completely ruined."[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196797-159) Leonardo, instead of using the reliable technique of fresco, had used tempera over a [ground](/wiki/Ground_(art) "Ground (art)") that was mainly [gesso](/wiki/Gesso "Gesso"), resulting in a surface subject to mould and to flaking.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196798-160) Despite this, the painting remains one of the most reproduced works of art; countless copies have been made in various mediums.

Toward the end of this period, in 1498 Leonardo's trompe-l'œil decoration of the [Sala delle Asse](/wiki/Sala_delle_Asse "Sala delle Asse") was painted for the Duke of Milan in the [Castello Sforzesco](/wiki/Castello_Sforzesco "Castello Sforzesco").

### Paintings of the 1500s

In 1505, Leonardo was commissioned to paint *The Battle of Anghiari* in the [Salone dei Cinquecento](/wiki/Salone_dei_Cinquecento "Salone dei Cinquecento") ("Hall of the Five Hundred") in the [Palazzo Vecchio](/wiki/Palazzo_Vecchio "Palazzo Vecchio"), Florence. Leonardo devised a dynamic composition depicting four men riding raging war horses engaged in a battle for possession of a standard, at the [Battle of Anghiari](/wiki/Battle_of_Anghiari "Battle of Anghiari") in 1440. Michelangelo was assigned the opposite wall to depict the [Battle of Cascina](/wiki/Battle_of_Cascina "Battle of Cascina"). Leonardo's painting deteriorated rapidly and is now known from a copy by [Rubens](/wiki/Rubens "Rubens").[[120]](#cite_note-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Mona_Lisa%2C_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci%2C_from_C2RMF_retouched.jpg/250px-Mona_Lisa%2C_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci%2C_from_C2RMF_retouched.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mona_Lisa,_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci,_from_C2RMF_retouched.jpg)

*[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")* or *La Gioconda* c. 1503–1516,[[d 8]](#cite_note-162) [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre"), Paris

Among the works created by Leonardo in the 16th century is the small portrait known as the *[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")* or *La Gioconda*, the laughing one. In the present era, it is arguably the most famous painting in the world. Its fame rests, in particular, on the elusive smile on the woman's face, its mysterious quality perhaps due to the subtly shadowed corners of the mouth and eyes such that the exact nature of the smile cannot be determined. The shadowy quality for which the work is renowned came to be called *[sfumato](/wiki/Sfumato "Sfumato"),* or "Leonardo's smoke". Vasari wrote that the smile was "so pleasing that it seems more divine than human, and it was considered a wondrous thing that it was as lively as the smile of the living original."[[‡ 9]](#cite_note-163)

Other characteristics of the painting are the unadorned dress, in which the eyes and hands have no competition from other details; the dramatic landscape background, in which the world seems to be in a state of flux; the subdued colouring; and the extremely smooth nature of the painterly technique, employing [oils](/wiki/Oil_paint "Oil paint") laid on much like [tempera](/wiki/Tempera "Tempera"), and blended on the surface so that the brushstrokes are indistinguishable.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman1975144-164) Vasari expressed that the painting's quality would make even "the most confident master ... despair and lose heart."[[‡ 10]](#cite_note-165) The perfect state of preservation and the fact that there is no sign of repair or overpainting is rare in a panel painting of this date.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa1967103-166)

In the painting *[Virgin and Child with Saint Anne](/wiki/The_Virgin_and_Child_with_Saint_Anne_(Leonardo) "The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne (Leonardo)")*, the composition again picks up the theme of figures in a landscape, which Wasserman describes as "breathtakingly beautiful"[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman1975150-167) and harkens back to the *Saint Jerome* with the figure set at an oblique angle. What makes this painting unusual is that there are two obliquely set figures superimposed. Mary is seated on the knee of her mother, Saint Anne. She leans forward to restrain the Christ Child as he plays roughly with a lamb, the sign of his own impending sacrifice.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) This painting, which was copied many times, influenced Michelangelo, Raphael, and [Andrea del Sarto](/wiki/Andrea_del_Sarto "Andrea del Sarto"),[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa1967109-168) and through them [Pontormo](/wiki/Pontormo "Pontormo") and [Correggio](/wiki/Correggio "Correggio"). The trends in composition were adopted in particular by the Venetian painters [Tintoretto](/wiki/Tintoretto "Tintoretto") and [Veronese](/wiki/Paolo_Veronese "Paolo Veronese").

## Drawings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Head_of_a_Warrior_-_Da_Vinci_1_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Head_of_a_Warrior_-_Da_Vinci_1_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Head_of_a_Warrior_-_Da_Vinci_1_(cropped).jpg)

*Antique Warrior in Profile*, c. 1472. British Museum, London

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_presumed_self-portrait_-_WGA12798.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_presumed_self-portrait_-_WGA12798.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_presumed_self-portrait_-_WGA12798.jpg)

Presumed [self-portrait of Leonardo](/wiki/Portrait_of_a_Man_in_Red_Chalk "Portrait of a Man in Red Chalk") (c. 1510) at the [Royal Library](/wiki/Royal_Library_of_Turin "Royal Library of Turin") of [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), Italy

Leonardo was a prolific draughtsman, keeping journals full of small sketches and detailed drawings recording all manner of things that took his attention. As well as the journals there exist many studies for paintings, some of which can be identified as preparatory to particular works such as *The Adoration of the Magi*, *The Virgin of the Rocks* and *The Last Supper*.[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169) His earliest dated drawing is a *Landscape of the Arno Valley*, 1473, which shows the river, the mountains, [Montelupo](/wiki/Montelupo_Fiorentino "Montelupo Fiorentino") Castle and the farmlands beyond it in great detail.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46)[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169)[[aa]](#cite_note-170)

Among his famous drawings are the *[Vitruvian Man](/wiki/Vitruvian_Man "Vitruvian Man")*, a study of the proportions of the human body; the *Head of an Angel*, for *[The Virgin of the Rocks](/wiki/The_Virgin_of_the_Rocks "The Virgin of the Rocks")* in the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre"); a botanical study of *Star of Bethlehem*; and a large drawing (160 × 100 cm) in black chalk on coloured paper of *[The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne and Saint John the Baptist](/wiki/The_Virgin_and_Child_with_Saint_Anne_and_Saint_John_the_Baptist "The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne and Saint John the Baptist")* in the National Gallery, London.[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169) This drawing employs the subtle *[sfumato](/wiki/Sfumato "Sfumato")* technique of shading, in the manner of the *Mona Lisa*. It is thought that Leonardo never made a painting from it, the closest similarity being to *[The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne](/wiki/The_Virgin_and_Child_with_Saint_Anne_(Leonardo) "The Virgin and Child with Saint Anne (Leonardo)")* in the Louvre.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa1967102-171)

Other drawings of interest include numerous studies generally referred to as "caricatures" because, although exaggerated, they appear to be based upon observation of live models. Vasari relates that Leonardo would look for interesting faces in public to use as models for some of his work.[[‡ 7]](#cite_note-:0-157) There are numerous studies of beautiful young men, often associated with Salaì, with the rare and much admired facial feature, the so-called "Grecian profile".[[ab]](#cite_note-172) These faces are often contrasted with that of a warrior.[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169) Salaì is often depicted in fancy-dress costume. Leonardo is known to have designed sets for pageants with which these may be associated. Other, often meticulous, drawings show studies of drapery. A marked development in Leonardo's ability to draw drapery occurred in his early works. Another often-reproduced drawing is a macabre sketch that was done by Leonardo in Florence in 1479 showing the body of [Bernardo Baroncelli](/wiki/Bernardo_Baroncelli "Bernardo Baroncelli"), hanged in connection with the murder of [Giuliano](/wiki/Giuliano_de%27_Medici "Giuliano de' Medici"), brother of Lorenzo de' Medici, in the [Pazzi conspiracy](/wiki/Pazzi_conspiracy "Pazzi conspiracy").[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169) In his notes, Leonardo recorded the colours of the robes that Baroncelli was wearing when he died.

Like the two contemporary architects [Donato Bramante](/wiki/Donato_Bramante "Donato Bramante") (who designed the [Belvedere Courtyard](/wiki/Cortile_del_Belvedere "Cortile del Belvedere")) and [Antonio da Sangallo the Elder](/wiki/Antonio_da_Sangallo_the_Elder "Antonio da Sangallo the Elder"), Leonardo experimented with designs for centrally planned churches, some of which appear in his journals, as both plans and views, although none was ever realised.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci19779–20-51)[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartt1970391–392-173)

## Journals and notes

See also: [List of works by Leonardo da Vinci § Manuscripts](/wiki/List_of_works_by_Leonardo_da_Vinci#Manuscripts "List of works by Leonardo da Vinci")

[Renaissance humanism](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") recognised no mutually exclusive polarities between the sciences and the arts, and Leonardo's studies in science and engineering are sometimes considered as impressive and innovative as his artistic work.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) These studies were recorded in 13,000 pages of notes and drawings, which fuse art and [natural philosophy](/wiki/Natural_philosophy "Natural philosophy") (the forerunner of modern science). They were made and maintained daily throughout Leonardo's life and travels, as he made continual observations of the world around him.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) Leonardo's notes and drawings display an enormous range of interests and preoccupations, some as mundane as lists of groceries and people who owed him money and some as intriguing as designs for wings and shoes for walking on water. There are compositions for paintings, studies of details and drapery, studies of faces and emotions, of animals, babies, dissections, plant studies, rock formations, whirlpools, war machines, flying machines and architecture.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Studies_of_the_foetus_in_the_womb.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Studies_of_the_foetus_in_the_womb.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_Studies_of_the_foetus_in_the_womb.jpg)

A page showing [Leonardo's study of a foetus in the womb](/wiki/Studies_of_the_Fetus_in_the_Womb "Studies of the Fetus in the Womb") (c. 1510), Royal Library, [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle")

These notebooks – originally loose papers of different types and sizes – were largely entrusted to Leonardo's pupil and heir Francesco Melzi after the master's death.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972169-174) These were to be published, a task of overwhelming difficulty because of its scope and Leonardo's idiosyncratic writing.[[129]](#cite_note-KDK-175) Some of Leonardo's drawings were copied by an anonymous Milanese artist for a planned treatise on art (*[Codex Huygens](/wiki/Codex_Huygens "Codex Huygens")*, c. 1570).[[130]](#cite_note-176) After Melzi's death in 1570, the collection passed to his son, the lawyer Orazio, who initially took little interest in the journals.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972169-174) In 1587, a Melzi household tutor named Lelio Gavardi took 13 of the manuscripts to Pisa; there, the architect [Giovanni Magenta](/wiki/Giovanni_Magenta "Giovanni Magenta") reproached Gavardi for having taken the manuscripts illicitly and returned them to Orazio. Having many more such works in his possession, Orazio gifted the volumes to Magenta. News spread of these lost works of Leonardo's, and Orazio retrieved seven of the 13 manuscripts, which he then gave to [Pompeo Leoni](/wiki/Pompeo_Leoni "Pompeo Leoni") for publication in two volumes; one of these was the *[Codex Atlanticus](/wiki/Codex_Atlanticus "Codex Atlanticus").* The other six works had been distributed to a few others.[[131]](#cite_note-177) After Orazio's death, his heirs sold the rest of Leonardo's possessions, and thus began their dispersal.[[132]](#cite_note-178)

Some works have found their way into major collections such as the Royal Library at [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle"), the [Louvre](/wiki/Louvre "Louvre"), the [Biblioteca Nacional de España](/wiki/Biblioteca_Nacional_de_Espa%C3%B1a "Biblioteca Nacional de España"), the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum"), the [Biblioteca Ambrosiana](/wiki/Biblioteca_Ambrosiana "Biblioteca Ambrosiana") in Milan, which holds the 12-volume Codex Atlanticus, and the [British Library](/wiki/British_Library "British Library") in London, which has put a selection from the [Codex Arundel](/wiki/Codex_Arundel "Codex Arundel") (BL Arundel MS 263) online.[[133]](#cite_note-179) Works have also been at [Holkham Hall](/wiki/Holkham_Hall "Holkham Hall"), the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), and in the private hands of [John Nicholas Brown I](/wiki/John_Nicholas_Brown_I "John Nicholas Brown I") and [Robert Lehman](/wiki/Robert_Lehman "Robert Lehman").[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972169-174) The [Codex Leicester](/wiki/Codex_Leicester "Codex Leicester") is the only privately owned major scientific work of Leonardo; it is owned by [Bill Gates](/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") and displayed once a year in different cities around the world.

Most of Leonardo's writings are in [mirror-image](/wiki/Mirror_writing "Mirror writing") cursive.[[46]](#cite_note-Polidoro-60)[[134]](#cite_note-Taylor-180) Since Leonardo wrote with his left hand, it was probably easier for him to write from right to left.[[135]](#cite_note-181)[[ac]](#cite_note-184) Leonardo used a variety of shorthand and symbols, and states in his notes that he intended to prepare them for publication.[[134]](#cite_note-Taylor-180) In many cases a single topic is covered in detail in both words and pictures on a single sheet, together conveying information that would not be lost if the pages were published out of order.[[138]](#cite_note-185) Why they were not published during Leonardo's lifetime is unknown.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)

## Science and inventions

Main article: [Science and inventions of Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Science_and_inventions_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Science and inventions of Leonardo da Vinci")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/De_divina_proportione_-_Vigintisex_Basium_Planum_Vacuum.jpg/250px-De_divina_proportione_-_Vigintisex_Basium_Planum_Vacuum.jpg)](/wiki/File:De_divina_proportione_-_Vigintisex_Basium_Planum_Vacuum.jpg)

[Rhombicuboctahedron](/wiki/Rhombicuboctahedron "Rhombicuboctahedron") as published in [Pacioli's](/wiki/Luca_Pacioli "Luca Pacioli") *[Divina proportione](/wiki/Divina_proportione "Divina proportione")* (1509)

Leonardo's approach to science was observational: he tried to understand a phenomenon by describing and depicting it in utmost detail and did not emphasise experiments or theoretical explanation. Since he lacked formal education in [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") and mathematics, contemporary scholars mostly ignored Leonardo the scientist, although he did teach himself Latin. His keen observations in many areas were noted, such as when he wrote "Il sole non si muove" ("The Sun does not move").[[139]](#cite_note-186)

In the 1490s he studied mathematics under Luca Pacioli and prepared a series of drawings of regular solids in a skeletal form to be engraved as plates for Pacioli's book *[Divina proportione](/wiki/Divina_proportione "Divina proportione")*, published in 1509.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) While living in Milan, he studied light from the summit of [Monte Rosa](/wiki/Monte_Rosa "Monte Rosa").[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972145-90) Scientific writings in his notebook on fossils have been considered as influential on [early palaeontology](/wiki/Palaeontology#History "Palaeontology").[[140]](#cite_note-187)

The content of his journals suggest that he was planning a series of treatises on a variety of subjects. A coherent treatise on [anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") is said to have been observed during a visit by Cardinal Louis d'Aragon's secretary in 1517.[[141]](#cite_note-188) Aspects of his work on the studies of anatomy, light and the landscape were assembled for publication by Melzi and eventually published as *[A Treatise on Painting](/wiki/Codex_Urbinas "Codex Urbinas")* in France and Italy in 1651 and Germany in 1724,[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa1967117-189) with engravings based upon drawings by the Classical painter [Nicolas Poussin](/wiki/Nicolas_Poussin "Nicolas Poussin").[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeydenreich2020-7) According to Arasse, the treatise, which in France went into 62 editions in 50 years, caused Leonardo to be seen as "the precursor of French academic thought on art."[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)

While Leonardo's experimentation followed scientific methods, a recent and exhaustive analysis of Leonardo as a scientist by Fritjof Capra argues that Leonardo was a fundamentally different kind of scientist from [Galileo](/wiki/Galileo "Galileo"), [Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") and other scientists who followed him in that, as a "[Renaissance Man](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath")", his theorising and hypothesising integrated the arts and particularly painting.[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECapra2007xvii–xx-190)

### Anatomy and physiology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_RCIN_919000%2C_Verso_The_bones_and_muscles_of_the_arm_c.1510-11.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_RCIN_919000%2C_Verso_The_bones_and_muscles_of_the_arm_c.1510-11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_RCIN_919000,_Verso_The_bones_and_muscles_of_the_arm_c.1510-11.jpg)

Anatomical study of the arm (c. 1510)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Leonardo_Da_Vinci%27s_Brain_Physiology.jpg/250px-Leonardo_Da_Vinci%27s_Brain_Physiology.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_Da_Vinci%27s_Brain_Physiology.jpg)

Leonardo's physiological sketch of the human brain and skull (c. 1510)

Leonardo started his study of the [anatomy](/wiki/Anatomy "Anatomy") of the [human body](/wiki/Human_body "Human body") under the apprenticeship of Verrocchio, who demanded that his students develop a deep knowledge of the subject. As an artist, he quickly became master of *topographic anatomy*, drawing many studies of [muscles](/wiki/Muscle "Muscle"), [tendons](/wiki/Tendon "Tendon") and other visible anatomical features.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a successful artist, Leonardo was given permission to [dissect](/wiki/Dissection "Dissection") human corpses at the [Hospital of Santa Maria Nuova](/wiki/Hospital_of_Santa_Maria_Nuova "Hospital of Santa Maria Nuova") in Florence and later at hospitals in Milan and Rome. From 1510 to 1511 he collaborated in his studies with the doctor [Marcantonio della Torre](/wiki/Marcantonio_della_Torre "Marcantonio della Torre"), professor of Anatomy at the [University of Pavia](/wiki/University_of_Pavia "University of Pavia").[[144]](#cite_note-191) Leonardo made more than 240 detailed drawings and wrote about 13,000 words toward a treatise on anatomy.[[145]](#cite_note-Sooke-192) Only a small amount of the material on anatomy was published in Leonardo's *Treatise on Painting*.[[129]](#cite_note-KDK-175) During the time that Melzi was ordering the material into chapters for publication, they were examined by anatomists and artists, including [Vasari](/wiki/Giorgio_Vasari "Giorgio Vasari"), [Cellini](/wiki/Benvenuto_Cellini "Benvenuto Cellini") and [Albrecht Dürer](/wiki/Albrecht_D%C3%BCrer "Albrecht Dürer"), who made drawings from them.[[129]](#cite_note-KDK-175)

Leonardo's anatomical drawings include many studies of the [human skeleton](/wiki/Human_skeleton "Human skeleton"), its parts, and the muscles and sinews. He studied the mechanical functions of the skeleton and the muscular forces that are applied to it in a manner that prefigured the modern science of [biomechanics](/wiki/Biomechanics "Biomechanics").[[146]](#cite_note-Mason-193) He drew the heart and [vascular system](/wiki/Circulatory_system "Circulatory system"), the [sex organs](/wiki/Sex_organs "Sex organs") and other internal organs, making one of the first scientific drawings of a [foetus](/wiki/Foetus "Foetus") *in utero*.[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169) The drawings and notation are far ahead of their time, and if published would undoubtedly have made a major contribution to medical science.[[145]](#cite_note-Sooke-192)

Leonardo also closely observed and recorded the effects of age and of human emotion on the physiology, studying in particular the effects of age. He drew many figures who had significant facial deformities or signs of illness.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169) Leonardo also studied and drew the anatomy of many animals, dissecting cows, birds, monkeys, bears, and frogs, and comparing in his drawings their anatomical structure with that of humans. He also made studies of horses.[[125]](#cite_note-Popham-169)

Leonardo's dissections and documentation of muscles, nerves, and vessels helped to describe the physiology and mechanics of movement. He attempted to identify the source of 'emotions' and their expression. He found it difficult to incorporate the prevailing system and theories of [bodily humours](/wiki/Humorism "Humorism"), but eventually he abandoned these physiological explanations of bodily functions. He made the observations that humours were not located in cerebral spaces or [ventricles](/wiki/Ventricular_system "Ventricular system"). He documented that the humours were not contained in the heart or the liver, and that it was the heart that defined the circulatory system. He was the first to define [atherosclerosis](/wiki/Atherosclerosis "Atherosclerosis") and liver [cirrhosis](/wiki/Cirrhosis "Cirrhosis"). He created models of the cerebral ventricles with the use of melted wax and constructed a glass [aorta](/wiki/Aorta "Aorta") to observe the circulation of blood through the aortic valve by using water and grass seed to watch flow patterns.[[147]](#cite_note-Jones2012-194)

### Engineering and inventions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Leonardo_da_vinci%2C_Drawing_of_a_flying_machine.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_vinci%2C_Drawing_of_a_flying_machine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_vinci,_Drawing_of_a_flying_machine.jpg)

A design for a flying machine (c. 1488), first presented in the *[Codex on the Flight of Birds](/wiki/Codex_on_the_Flight_of_Birds "Codex on the Flight of Birds")*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Leonardo_da_Vinci_helicopter.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_helicopter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_helicopter.jpg)

An *[aerial screw](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_aerial_screw "Leonardo's aerial screw")* (c. 1489), suggestive of a helicopter, from the *[Codex Atlanticus](/wiki/Codex_Atlanticus "Codex Atlanticus")*

During his lifetime, Leonardo was also valued as an engineer. With the same rational and analytical approach that moved him to represent the human body and to investigate anatomy, Leonardo studied and designed many machines and devices. He drew their "anatomy" with unparalleled mastery, producing the first form of the modern technical drawing, including a perfected "exploded view" technique, to represent internal components. Those studies and projects collected in his codices fill more than 5,000 pages.[[148]](#cite_note-guarnieri1-195) In a letter of 1482 to the lord of Milan [Ludovico il Moro](/wiki/Ludovico_il_Moro "Ludovico il Moro"), he wrote that he could create all sorts of machines both for the protection of a city and for siege. When he fled from Milan to Venice in 1499, he found employment as an engineer and devised a system of moveable barricades to protect the city from attack. In 1502, he created a scheme for diverting the flow of the Arno river, a project on which [Niccolò Machiavelli](/wiki/Niccol%C3%B2_Machiavelli "Niccolò Machiavelli") also worked.[[149]](#cite_note-196)[[150]](#cite_note-197) He continued to contemplate the canalisation of [Lombardy's plains](/wiki/Lombardy#Soils "Lombardy") while in Louis XII's company[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972145-90) and of the [Loire](/wiki/Loire "Loire") and its tributaries in the company of Francis I.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972164-198) Leonardo's journals include a vast number of inventions, both practical and impractical. They include [musical instruments](/wiki/Viola_organista "Viola organista"), [a mechanical knight](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_robot "Leonardo's robot"), hydraulic pumps, reversible crank mechanisms, finned mortar shells, and a [steam cannon](/wiki/Steam_cannon "Steam cannon").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46)[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_1860%2C0616.99%2C_Studies_of_military_tank-like_machines_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_1860%2C0616.99%2C_Studies_of_military_tank-like_machines_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leonardo_da_Vinci_-_1860,0616.99,_Studies_of_military_tank-like_machines_(cropped).jpg)

Leonardo's drawings of a scythed chariot and a [fighting vehicle](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_fighting_vehicle "Leonardo's fighting vehicle")

Leonardo was fascinated by the phenomenon of [flight](/wiki/History_of_aviation "History of aviation") for much of his life, producing many studies, including *[Codex on the Flight of Birds](/wiki/Codex_on_the_Flight_of_Birds "Codex on the Flight of Birds")* (c. 1505), as well as plans for several flying machines, such as a flapping [ornithopter](/wiki/Ornithopter "Ornithopter") and a machine with a helical [rotor](/wiki/Helicopter_rotor "Helicopter rotor").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47) In a 2003 documentary by British television station [Channel Four](/wiki/Channel_Four "Channel Four"), titled *Leonardo's Dream Machines*, various designs by Leonardo, such as a [parachute](/wiki/Parachute#Early_Renaissance "Parachute") and [a giant crossbow](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_crossbow "Leonardo's crossbow"), were interpreted and constructed.[[152]](#cite_note-199)[[153]](#cite_note-200) Some of those designs proved successful, whilst others fared less well when tested. Similarly, a team of engineers built ten machines designed by Leonardo in the 2009 American television series *[Doing DaVinci](/wiki/Doing_DaVinci "Doing DaVinci")*, including a [fighting vehicle](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_fighting_vehicle "Leonardo's fighting vehicle") and a [self-propelled cart](/wiki/Leonardo%27s_self-propelled_cart "Leonardo's self-propelled cart").

Research performed by [Marc van den Broek](/wiki/Marc_van_den_Broek "Marc van den Broek") revealed older prototypes for more than 100 inventions that are ascribed to Leonardo. Similarities between Leonardo's illustrations and drawings from the Middle Ages and from Ancient Greece and Rome, the Chinese and Persian Empires, and Egypt suggest that a large portion of Leonardo's inventions had been conceived before his lifetime. Leonardo's innovation was to combine different functions from existing drafts and set them into scenes that illustrated their utility. By reconstituting technical inventions he created something new.[[154]](#cite_note-201)

In his notebooks, Leonardo first stated the 'laws' of sliding [friction](/wiki/Friction "Friction") in 1493.[[155]](#cite_note-Hutchings-202) His inspiration for investigating friction came about in part from his study of [perpetual motion](/wiki/Perpetual_motion "Perpetual motion"), which he correctly concluded was not possible.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaacson2017194–197-203) His results were never published and the friction laws were not rediscovered until 1699 by [Guillaume Amontons](/wiki/Guillaume_Amontons "Guillaume Amontons"), with whose name they are now usually associated.[[‡ 7]](#cite_note-:0-157) For this contribution, Leonardo was named as the first of the 23 "Men of Tribology" by [Duncan Dowson](/wiki/Duncan_Dowson "Duncan Dowson").[[157]](#cite_note-204)

## Legacy

Further information: [Cultural references to Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Cultural_references_to_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Cultural references to Leonardo da Vinci") and [List of things named after Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/List_of_things_named_after_Leonardo_da_Vinci "List of things named after Leonardo da Vinci")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Statue_of_Leonardo_DaVinci_in_Uffizi_Alley%2C_Florence%2C_Italy.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Leonardo_DaVinci_in_Uffizi_Alley%2C_Florence%2C_Italy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Leonardo_DaVinci_in_Uffizi_Alley,_Florence,_Italy.jpg)

Statue outside the [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi"), Florence, by [Luigi Pampaloni](/wiki/Luigi_Pampaloni "Luigi Pampaloni") (1791–1847)

Although he had no formal academic training,[[158]](#cite_note-205) many historians and scholars regard Leonardo as the prime exemplar of the "[Universal Genius](/wiki/Universal_Genius "Universal Genius")" or "Renaissance Man", an individual of "unquenchable curiosity" and "feverishly inventive imagination."[[159]](#cite_note-HG-206) He is widely considered one of the most diversely talented individuals ever to have lived.[[160]](#cite_note-genius-207) According to art historian [Helen Gardner](/wiki/Helen_Gardner_(art_historian) "Helen Gardner (art historian)"), the scope and depth of his interests were without precedent in recorded history, and "his mind and [personality](/wiki/Personal_life_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Personal life of Leonardo da Vinci") seem to us superhuman, while the man himself mysterious and remote."[[159]](#cite_note-HG-206) Scholars interpret his view of the world as being based in logic, though the empirical methods he used were unorthodox for his time.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci19778-208)

Leonardo's fame within his own lifetime was such that the King of France carried him away like a trophy, and was claimed to have supported him in his old age and held him in his arms as he died. Interest in Leonardo and his work has never diminished. Crowds still queue to see his best-known artworks, T-shirts still bear his most famous drawing, and writers continue to hail him as a genius while speculating about his private life, as well as about what one so intelligent actually believed in.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArasse1998-47)

The continued admiration that Leonardo commanded from painters, critics and historians is reflected in many other written tributes. [Baldassare Castiglione](/wiki/Baldassare_Castiglione "Baldassare Castiglione"), author of *[Il Cortegiano](/wiki/Il_Cortegiano "Il Cortegiano")* (*The Courtier*), wrote in 1528: "...Another of the greatest painters in this world looks down on this art in which he is unequalled..."[[162]](#cite_note-209) while the biographer known as "Anonimo Gaddiano" wrote, c. 1540: "His genius was so rare and universal that it can be said that nature worked a miracle on his behalf..."[[163]](#cite_note-210) Vasari, in his *[Lives of the Artists](/wiki/Lives_of_the_Artists "Lives of the Artists")* (1568), opens his chapter on Leonardo:[[‡ 11]](#cite_note-211)

> In the normal course of events many men and women are born with remarkable talents; but occasionally, in a way that transcends nature, a single person is marvellously endowed by Heaven with beauty, grace and talent in such abundance that he leaves other men far behind, all his actions seem inspired and indeed everything he does clearly comes from God rather than from human skill. Everyone acknowledged that this was true of Leonardo da Vinci, an artist of outstanding physical beauty, who displayed infinite grace in everything that he did and who cultivated his genius so brilliantly that all problems he studied he solved with ease.

The 19th century brought a particular admiration for Leonardo's genius, causing [Henry Fuseli](/wiki/Henry_Fuseli "Henry Fuseli") to write in 1801: "Such was the dawn of modern art, when Leonardo da Vinci broke forth with a splendour that distanced former excellence: made up of all the elements that constitute the essence of genius..."[[164]](#cite_note-212) This is echoed by A. E. Rio who wrote in 1861: "He towered above all other artists through the strength and the nobility of his talents."[[165]](#cite_note-213)

By the 19th century, the scope of Leonardo's notebooks was known, as well as his paintings. [Hippolyte Taine](/wiki/Hippolyte_Taine "Hippolyte Taine") wrote in 1866: "There may not be in the world an example of another genius so universal, so incapable of fulfilment, so full of yearning for the infinite, so naturally refined, so far ahead of his own century and the following centuries."[[166]](#cite_note-214)

Art historian [Bernard Berenson](/wiki/Bernard_Berenson "Bernard Berenson") wrote in 1896:

> Leonardo is the one artist of whom it may be said with perfect literalness: Nothing that he touched but turned into a thing of eternal beauty. Whether it be the cross section of a skull, the structure of a weed, or a study of muscles, he, with his feeling for line and for light and shade, forever transmuted it into life-communicating values.[[167]](#cite_note-215)

The interest in Leonardo's genius has continued unabated; experts study and translate his writings, analyse his paintings using scientific techniques, argue over attributions and search for works which have been recorded but never found.[[168]](#cite_note-216) Liana Bortolon, writing in 1967, said:

> Because of the multiplicity of interests that spurred him to pursue every field of knowledge...Leonardo can be considered, quite rightly, to have been the universal genius par excellence, and with all the disquieting overtones inherent in that term. Man is as uncomfortable today, faced with a genius, as he was in the 16th century. Five centuries have passed, yet we still view Leonardo with awe.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46)

The [Elmer Belt Library of Vinciana](/wiki/Elmer_Belt_Library_of_Vinciana "Elmer Belt Library of Vinciana") is a special collection at the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles").[[169]](#cite_note-217)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Museo_leonardiano_di_vinci%2C_04.JPG/250px-Museo_leonardiano_di_vinci%2C_04.JPG)](/wiki/File:Museo_leonardiano_di_vinci,_04.JPG)

[Leonardo Museum in Vinci](/wiki/Leonardian_Museum_of_Vinci "Leonardian Museum of Vinci"), which houses a large collection of models constructed on the basis of Leonardo's drawings

Twenty-first-century author [Walter Isaacson](/wiki/Walter_Isaacson "Walter Isaacson") based much of [his biography of Leonardo](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci_(Isaacson_book) "Leonardo da Vinci (Isaacson book)")[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaacson2017-137) on thousands of notebook entries, studying the personal notes, sketches, budget notations, and musings of the man whom he considers the greatest of innovators. Isaacson was surprised to discover a "fun, joyous" side of Leonardo in addition to his limitless curiosity and creative genius.[[170]](#cite_note-218)

On the 500th anniversary of Leonardo's death, the Louvre in Paris arranged for the largest ever single exhibit of his work, called *Leonardo*, between November 2019 and February 2020. The exhibit includes more than 100 paintings, drawings and notebooks. Eleven of the paintings that Leonardo completed in his lifetime were included. Five of these are owned by the Louvre, but the *Mona Lisa* was not included because it is in such great demand among general visitors to the Louvre; it remains on display in its gallery. *Vitruvian Man*, however, is on display following a legal battle with its owner, the [Gallerie dell'Accademia](/wiki/Gallerie_dell%27Accademia "Gallerie dell'Accademia") in Venice. *[Salvator Mundi](/wiki/Salvator_Mundi_(Leonardo) "Salvator Mundi (Leonardo)")*[[ad]](#cite_note-221) was also not included because its Saudi owner did not agree to lease the work.[[173]](#cite_note-222)[[174]](#cite_note-223)

The *Mona Lisa*, considered Leonardo's [magnum opus](/wiki/Magnum_opus "Magnum opus"), is often regarded as the most famous portrait ever made.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKemp2003-6)[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETurner19933-224) *The Last Supper* is the most reproduced religious painting of all time,[[159]](#cite_note-HG-206) and Leonardo's *Vitruvian Man* drawing is also considered a [cultural icon](/wiki/Cultural_icon "Cultural icon").[[176]](#cite_note-225)

More than a decade of analysis of Leonardo's [genetic genealogy](/wiki/Genetic_genealogy "Genetic genealogy"), conducted by [Alessandro Vezzosi](/wiki/Alessandro_Vezzosi "Alessandro Vezzosi") and Agnese Sabato, came to a conclusion in mid-2021. It was determined that the artist has 14 living male relatives. The work could also help determine the authenticity of remains thought to belong to Leonardo.[[177]](#cite_note-226)

## Location of remains

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Tombe_de_L%C3%A9onard_de_Vinci.JPG/250px-Tombe_de_L%C3%A9onard_de_Vinci.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tombe_de_L%C3%A9onard_de_Vinci.JPG)

Tomb in the chapel of Saint Hubert at the [Château d'Amboise](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_d%27Amboise "Château d'Amboise") where a plaque describes it as the presumed site of Leonardo's remains

While Leonardo was certainly buried in the [collegiate church](/wiki/Collegiate_church "Collegiate church") of Saint Florentin at the Château d'Amboise in 12 August 1519, the current location of his remains is unclear.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage502_502]-227)[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaacson2017515-228) Much of Château d'Amboise was damaged during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), leading to the church's demolition in 1802.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage502_502]-227) Some of the graves were destroyed in the process, scattering the bones interred there and thereby leaving the whereabouts of Leonardo's remains subject to dispute; a gardener may have even buried some in the corner of the courtyard.[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage502_502]-227)

In 1863, fine-arts [inspector general](/wiki/Inspector_general "Inspector general") [Arsène Houssaye](/wiki/Ars%C3%A8ne_Houssaye "Arsène Houssaye") received an imperial commission to excavate the site and discovered a partially complete skeleton with a bronze ring on one finger, white hair, and stone fragments bearing the inscriptions "EO", "AR", "DUS", and "VINC" – interpreted as forming "Leonardus Vinci".[[94]](#cite_note-Florentine-123)[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage502_502]-227)[[180]](#cite_note-Ouest-229) The skull's eight teeth correspond to someone of approximately the appropriate age, and a silver shield found near the bones depicts a beardless [Francis I](/wiki/Francis_I_of_France "Francis I of France"), corresponding to the king's appearance during Leonardo's time in France.[[180]](#cite_note-Ouest-229)

Houssaye postulated that the unusually large skull was an indicator of Leonardo's intelligence; author [Charles Nicholl](/wiki/Charles_Nicholl_(author) "Charles Nicholl (author)") describes this as a "dubious [phrenological](/wiki/Phrenological "Phrenological") deduction".[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage502_502]-227) At the same time, Houssaye noted some issues with his observations, including that the feet were turned toward the [high altar](/wiki/High_altar "High altar"), a practice generally reserved for [laymen](/wiki/Laymen "Laymen"), and that the skeleton of 1.73 metres (5.7 ft) seemed too short.[[180]](#cite_note-Ouest-229)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") – [see discussion](/wiki/Talk:Leonardo_da_Vinci#Houssaye_and_1.73_m/5.8ft_inconsistency "Talk:Leonardo da Vinci")*] Art historian [Mary Margaret Heaton](/wiki/Mary_Margaret_Heaton "Mary Margaret Heaton") wrote in 1874 that the height would be appropriate for Leonardo.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeaton1874[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidfqUaAAAAYAAJpgPA204_204]"The_skeleton,_which_measured_five_feet_eight_inches,_accords_with_the_height_of_Leonardo_da_Vinci._The_skull_might_have_served_for_the_model_of_the_portrait_Leonardo_drew_of_himself_in_red_chalk_a_few_years_before_his_death."-230) The skull was allegedly presented to [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") before being returned to the Château d'Amboise, where they were re-interred in the chapel of Saint Hubert in 1874.[[180]](#cite_note-Ouest-229)[[182]](#cite_note-telegraph-231) A plaque above the tomb states that its contents are only presumed to be those of Leonardo.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaacson2017515-228)

It has since been theorised that the folding of the skeleton's right arm over the head may correspond to the paralysis of Leonardo's right hand.[[83]](#cite_note-paralysis-106)[[89]](#cite_note-neurology-115)[[180]](#cite_note-Ouest-229) In 2016, it was announced that DNA tests would be conducted to determine whether the attribution is correct.[[182]](#cite_note-telegraph-231) The DNA of the remains will be compared to that of samples collected from Leonardo's work and his half-brother Domenico's descendants;[[182]](#cite_note-telegraph-231) it may also be [sequenced](/wiki/DNA_sequencing "DNA sequencing").[[183]](#cite_note-232)

In 2019, documents were published revealing that Houssaye had kept the ring and a lock of hair. In 1925, his great-grandson sold these to an American collector. Sixty years later, another American acquired them, leading to their being displayed at the [Leonardo Museum in Vinci](/wiki/Museo_Ideale_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Museo Ideale Leonardo da Vinci") beginning on 2 May 2019, the 500th anniversary of the artist's death.[[94]](#cite_note-Florentine-123)[[184]](#cite_note-233)

## Notes

**General**

1. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Birthplace_3-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Birthplace_3-1) See [Nicholl (2005](#CITEREFNicholl2005), pp. 17–20) and [Bambach (2019](#CITEREFBambach2019), p. 24) for further information on the dispute and uncertainty surrounding Leonardo's exact birthplace.
2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-IPA_4-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-IPA_4-1) [/ˌliːəˈnɑːrdoʊ də ˈvɪntʃi, ˌliːoʊˈ-, ˌleɪoʊˈ-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*LEE-ə-NAR-doh də VIN-chee, LEE-oh-, LAY-oh-*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); Italian: [[leoˈnardo di ˌsɛr ˈpjɛːro da (v)ˈvintʃi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Leonardo_di_ser_Piero_da_Vinci.ogg "File:It-Leonardo di ser Piero da Vinci.ogg").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** In this [Renaissance Italian](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") name, the name *da Vinci* is an indicator of birthplace or [paternity](/wiki/Patronymic "Patronymic"), not a [family name](/wiki/Family_name "Family name"); the person is properly referred to by the [given name](/wiki/Given_name "Given name"), Leonardo.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-ser_15-0)** The inclusion of the title *ser* (shortening of Italian *messer* or *messere*, title of courtesy prefixed to the first name) indicates that Leonardo's father was a gentleman (a low-ranking nobleman).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-21)** The diary of his paternal grandfather Ser Antonio relays a precise account: "There was born to me a grandson, son of [Ser Piero](/w/index.php?title=Piero_da_Vinci&action=edit&redlink=1 "Piero da Vinci (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piero_da_Vinci "fr:Piero da Vinci")], on 15 April, a Saturday, at the third hour of the night."[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVezzosi199713-18)[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196783-19) Ser Antonio records Leonardo being baptised the following day by Piero di Bartolomeo at the [parish](/wiki/Parish "Parish") of [Santa Croce](/w/index.php?title=Chiesa_di_Santa_Croce_(Vinci)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chiesa di Santa Croce (Vinci) (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chiesa_di_Santa_Croce_(Vinci) "it:Chiesa di Santa Croce (Vinci)")].[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicholl2005[httpsarchiveorgdetailsleonardodavinci00charpage20_20]-20)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** It has been suggested that Caterina may have been a slave from the Middle East "or at least, from the Mediterranean" or even of Chinese descent. According to art critic [Alessandro Vezzosi](/wiki/Alessandro_Vezzosi "Alessandro Vezzosi"), head of the [Leonardo Museum in Vinci](/wiki/Museo_Ideale_Leonardo_da_Vinci "Museo Ideale Leonardo da Vinci"), there is evidence that Piero owned a slave called Caterina.[[20]](#cite_note-25) The reconstruction of one of Leonardo's fingerprints shows a pattern that matches 60% of people of Middle Eastern origin, suggesting the possibility that Leonardo may have had Middle Eastern blood. The claim is refuted by Simon Cole, associate professor of criminology, law and society at the University of California at Irvine: "You can't predict one person's race from these kinds of incidences, especially if looking at only one finger". More recently, historian [Martin Kemp](/wiki/Martin_Kemp_(art_historian) "Martin Kemp (art historian)"), after digging through overlooked archives and records in Italy, found evidence that Leonardo's mother was a young local woman identified as Caterina di Meo Lippi.[[21]](#cite_note-26)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** See [Kemp & Pallanti (2017](#CITEREFKempPallanti2017), pp. 65–66) for detailed table on Ser Piero's marriages.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-35)** He also never wrote about his father, except a passing note of his death in which he overstates his age by three years.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197211-33) Leonardo's siblings caused him difficulty after his father's death in a dispute over their inheritance.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMagnano2007138-34)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-44)** The humanist influence of Donatello's *[David](/wiki/David_(Donatello,_bronze) "David (Donatello, bronze)")* can be seen in Leonardo's late paintings, particularly *[John the Baptist](/wiki/St._John_the_Baptist_(Leonardo) "St. John the Baptist (Leonardo)")*.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHartt1970127–133-43)[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosci197713-42)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-50)** The "diverse arts" and technical skills of Medieval and Renaissance workshops are described in detail in the 12th-century text *On Divers Arts* by [Theophilus Presbyter](/wiki/Theophilus_Presbyter "Theophilus Presbyter") and in the early 15th-century text *Il Libro dell'Arte* by [Cennino Cennini](/wiki/Cennino_Cennini "Cennino Cennini").
11. **[^](#cite_ref-58)** That Leonardo joined the guild by this time is deduced from the record of payment made to the [Compagnia di San Luca](/wiki/Compagnia_di_San_Luca "Compagnia di San Luca") in the company's register, Libro Rosso A, 1472–1520, Accademia di Belle Arti.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa196783-19)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-61)** On the back he wrote: "I, staying with Anthony, am happy," possibly in reference to his father.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-64)** Leonardo later wrote in the margin of a journal, "the Medici made me and the Medici destroyed me."[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBortolon1967-46)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-82)** In 2005, the studio was rediscovered during the restoration of part of a building occupied for 100 years by the Department of Military Geography.[[63]](#cite_note-81)
15. **[^](#cite_ref-89)** Both works are lost. The entire composition of Michelangelo's painting is known from a copy by Aristotole da Sangallo, 1542.[[68]](#cite_note-87) Leonardo's painting is known only from preparatory sketches and several copies of the centre section, of which the best known, and probably least accurate, is by [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens").[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOttino_della_Chiesa1967106–107-88)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-96)** Pope Leo X is quoted as saying, "This man will never accomplish anything! He thinks of the end before the beginning!"[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972150-95)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-100)** There is no documentary basis for the frequently made claim that Leonardo was present at the meeting between Francis I and Leo X, which took place in Bologna from 11 to 14 December 1516.[[78]](#cite_note-99)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-104)** It is unknown for what occasion the mechanical lion was made, but it is believed to have greeted the King at his entry into [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") and perhaps was used for the peace talks between the French king and Pope Leo X in Bologna. A conjectural recreation of the lion has been made and is on display in the Museum of Bologna.[[81]](#cite_note-103)
19. **[^](#cite_ref-108)** Identified via its similarity to Leonardo's [presumed self-portrait](/wiki/Portrait_of_a_Man_in_Red_Chalk "Portrait of a Man in Red Chalk").[[84]](#cite_note-guardian2005-107)
20. **[^](#cite_ref-110)** "... Messer Lunardo Vinci [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] ... an old graybeard of more than 70 years ... showed His Excellency three pictures ... from whom, since he was then subject to a certain paralysis of the right hand, one could not expect any more good work."[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace1972163-109)
21. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-edict_114-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-edict_114-1) On the day of Leonardo's death, a royal edict was issued by the King at [Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Saint-Germain-en-Laye "Saint-Germain-en-Laye"), a two-day journey from Clos Lucé. This has been taken as evidence that King Francis cannot have been present at Leonardo's deathbed, but the edict was not signed by the King.[[92]](#cite_note-120)
22. **[^](#cite_ref-119)** This scene is portrayed in romantic paintings by [Ingres](/wiki/Jean-Auguste-Dominique_Ingres "Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres"), [Ménageot](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois-Guillaume_M%C3%A9nageot "François-Guillaume Ménageot") and other French artists, as well as [Angelica Kauffman](/wiki/Angelica_Kauffman "Angelica Kauffman").
23. **[^](#cite_ref-121)** Each of the sixty paupers were to have been awarded in accord with Leonardo's will.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWilliamson1974-67)
24. **[^](#cite_ref-141)** [Martin Kemp](/wiki/Martin_Kemp_(art_historian) "Martin Kemp (art historian)") states that "it is clear that Leonardo, whether he was active or not, was homosexual. But the question is, then, what do we do with that information? Is this profoundly expressed in his art? Is this a major explanatory mode? Over years of looking at Leonardo, I am not resistant to thinking of sexuality as a key but I have not found it particularly helpful."[[108]](#cite_note-140)
25. **[^](#cite_ref-143)** These qualities of Leonardo's works are discussed in [Hartt (1970](#CITEREFHartt1970), pp. 387–411).
26. **[^](#cite_ref-149)** The painting, which in the 18th century belonged to [Angelica Kauffman](/wiki/Angelica_Kauffman "Angelica Kauffman"), was later cut up. The two main sections were found in a junk shop and cobbler's shop and were reunited.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWasserman1975104–106-148) It is probable that outer parts of the composition are missing.
27. **[^](#cite_ref-170)** This work is now in the collection of the [Uffizi](/wiki/Uffizi "Uffizi"), Drawing No. 8P.
28. **[^](#cite_ref-172)** The "Grecian profile" has a continuous straight line from forehead to nose-tip, the bridge of the nose being exceptionally high. It is a feature of many [Classical Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_sculpture "Ancient Greek sculpture") statues.
29. **[^](#cite_ref-184)** He also drew with his left hand, his [hatch](/wiki/Hatching "Hatching") strokes "slanting down from left to right – the natural stroke of a left-handed artist".[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWallace197231-182) He also sometimes wrote conventionally with his right hand.[[137]](#cite_note-183)
30. **[^](#cite_ref-221)** *[Salvator Mundi](/wiki/Salvator_Mundi_(Leonardo) "Salvator Mundi (Leonardo)")*, a painting by Leonardo depicting Jesus holding an orb, sold for a world record US$450.3 million at a [Christie's](/wiki/Christie%27s "Christie's") auction in New York, 15 November 2017.[[171]](#cite_note-219) The highest known sale price for any artwork was previously US$300 million, for [Willem de Kooning](/wiki/Willem_de_Kooning "Willem de Kooning")'s *[Interchange](/wiki/Interchange_(de_Kooning) "Interchange (de Kooning)")*, which was sold privately in September 2015.[[172]](#cite_note-fox-220) The highest price previously paid for a work of art at auction was for [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso")'s *[Les Femmes d'Alger](/wiki/Les_Femmes_d%27Alger "Les Femmes d'Alger")*, which sold for US$179.4 million in May 2015 at Christie's New York.[[172]](#cite_note-fox-220)
